---
title: "Widget gallery: live demos + how to launch each one per language"
---

[`docs/widgets.md`](widgets.md) lists the six ipywidgets categories cositos' example
widgets cover. This page makes that list concrete: **a live, in-browser demo of each
widget**, plus **the exact snippet that launches it from a real backend language**.

Every demo below runs the *unmodified* ESM from `examples/widgets/*.js` — the same file
Jupyter (via the anywidget frontend) and `@cositos/front` (web/WASM/Pluto) both load. The
demos on this page render with zero kernel, using `LocalChannel` — a loopback channel with
no backend (see [`docs/hosts.md`](hosts.md#localchannel--client-only-widgets)) — purely so
this page doesn't need a running Jupyter kernel to show what clicking the widget does.
**A real kernel changes only which `Channel` is plugged in** (`CommTransport` for Python,
`ijulia_transport()` for Julia, …); the ESM and the widget's on-screen behavior are
identical either way — see [`tutorials/polyglot-parity.qmd`](tutorials/polyglot-parity.qmd)
for the proof that both backends serialize to the same document.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
// Note the "../../": a dynamic import() inside a compiled OJS cell resolves relative to
// Quarto's own runtime module (site_libs/quarto-ojs/…), not to this page's URL — see
// tutorials/web-ojs.qmd for the full explanation.
front = await import("../../vendor/cositos-front/index.js")
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
function demo(esm, initialState) {
  return async () => {
    const { Model, LocalChannel, loadWidget, renderWidget } = front;
    const el = DOM.element("div", {
      style: "border:1px solid #ddd;border-radius:8px;padding:1rem;max-width:20rem;",
    });
    const model = new Model(initialState, new LocalChannel());
    const widget = await loadWidget(esm);
    await renderWidget(widget, { model, el });
    return el;
  };
}
```

## The launch pattern

Every widget below is launched the same way, in every backend language: the host owns a
state object and the ESM string, and hands both to cositos, which wires up the comm
protocol. Only the ESM and the initial state change between widgets — swap them into
whichever snippet matches your kernel.

::: {.panel-tabset}

## Python

```{python}
# | eval: false
from cositos import Widget
from cositos.jupyter import CommTransport

ESM = "..."  # one of the examples/widgets/*.js files below
state = {"...": "..."}  # that widget's initial state, from the table below

widget = Widget(
    CommTransport(),
    get_state=lambda: dict(state),
    set_state=state.update,
)
widget  # last line of a cell: renders live
```

## Julia

```julia
using Cositos, IJulia

ESM = raw"""..."""  # one of the examples/widgets/*.js files below

state = Dict{String,Any}("..." => "...")  # that widget's initial state, from the table below
widget = Widget(
    Cositos.ijulia_transport();
    get_state = () -> copy(state),
    set_state = (d) -> merge!(state, d),
);

widget  # last line of a cell: renders live
```

:::

Clojure (Clay or the clojupyter crack), and the R/C# protocol-core-only status, are
covered per-language in [`tutorials/quickstart.qmd`](tutorials/quickstart.qmd) — that page
walks the *counter* widget end to end for every backend; this page is the *widget
catalog*, so each entry below only calls out the two live-today backends plus the ESM +
state you'd plug into them (or into the Clojure/Clay pattern from quickstart).

## The widgets

### IntSlider — numeric (ipywidgets: `IntSlider`, `FloatSlider`)

`examples/widgets/int_slider.js` — a range input whose `value` two-way-syncs with the
host.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
intSliderEsm = `
export default {
  render({ model, el }) {
    const input = document.createElement("input");
    input.type = "range";
    input.min = String(model.get("min") ?? 0);
    input.max = String(model.get("max") ?? 100);
    input.value = String(model.get("value") ?? 0);
    const label = document.createElement("span");
    const paint = () => {
      input.value = String(model.get("value"));
      label.textContent = " " + model.get("value");
    };
    paint();
    input.addEventListener("input", () => {
      model.set("value", Number(input.value));
      model.save_changes();
    });
    model.on("change:value", paint);
    el.appendChild(input);
    el.appendChild(label);
    return () => { input.remove(); label.remove(); };
  },
};
`
```

```{ojs}
intSlider = demo(intSliderEsm, { value: 10, min: 0, max: 100 })()
```

Initial state: `{ value: 10, min: 0, max: 100 }`.

### Checkbox — boolean (ipywidgets: `Checkbox`, `ToggleButton`)

`examples/widgets/checkbox.js` — a checkbox whose `value` two-way-syncs with the host.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
checkboxEsm = `
export default {
  render({ model, el }) {
    const input = document.createElement("input");
    input.type = "checkbox";
    const paint = () => { input.checked = Boolean(model.get("value")); };
    paint();
    input.addEventListener("change", () => {
      model.set("value", input.checked);
      model.save_changes();
    });
    model.on("change:value", paint);
    el.appendChild(input);
    return () => input.remove();
  },
};
`
```

```{ojs}
checkbox = demo(checkboxEsm, { value: false })()
```

Initial state: `{ value: false }`.

### Text — string (ipywidgets: `Text`, `Textarea`)

`examples/widgets/text.js` — a text input whose `value` two-way-syncs with the host.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
textEsm = `
export default {
  render({ model, el }) {
    const input = document.createElement("input");
    input.type = "text";
    const paint = () => {
      if (input.value !== model.get("value")) input.value = model.get("value") ?? "";
    };
    paint();
    input.addEventListener("input", () => {
      model.set("value", input.value);
      model.save_changes();
    });
    model.on("change:value", paint);
    el.appendChild(input);
    return () => input.remove();
  },
};
`
```

```{ojs}
text = demo(textEsm, { value: "hi" })()
```

Initial state: `{ value: "hi" }`.

### Button — button/event (ipywidgets: `Button`)

`examples/widgets/button.js` — clicking bumps a `clicks` state field *and* sends a
`custom` event, mirroring how ipywidgets' `Button` emits `on_click` rather than state.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
buttonEsm = `
export default {
  render({ model, el }) {
    const button = document.createElement("button");
    const paint = () => { button.textContent = model.get("description") ?? "Click"; };
    paint();
    button.addEventListener("click", () => {
      model.set("clicks", (model.get("clicks") ?? 0) + 1);
      model.save_changes();
      model.send({ event: "click" });
    });
    model.on("change:description", paint);
    el.appendChild(button);
    return () => button.remove();
  },
};
`
```

```{ojs}
button = demo(buttonEsm, { description: "Go", clicks: 0 })()
```

Initial state: `{ description: "Go", clicks: 0 }`. On a real backend, `clicks` and the
`custom` `{event: "click"}` message both arrive at the kernel — the host decides which
one it cares about.

### Dropdown — selection (ipywidgets: `Dropdown`, `Select`, `RadioButtons`)

`examples/widgets/dropdown.js` — a `<select>` whose `options` list and `value` both
sync with the host.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
dropdownEsm = `
export default {
  render({ model, el }) {
    const select = document.createElement("select");
    const build = () => {
      select.innerHTML = "";
      for (const opt of model.get("options") ?? []) {
        const o = document.createElement("option");
        o.value = String(opt);
        o.textContent = String(opt);
        select.appendChild(o);
      }
      select.value = String(model.get("value"));
    };
    build();
    select.addEventListener("change", () => {
      model.set("value", select.value);
      model.save_changes();
    });
    model.on("change:value", () => { select.value = String(model.get("value")); });
    model.on("change:options", build);
    el.appendChild(select);
    return () => select.remove();
  },
};
`
```

```{ojs}
dropdown = demo(dropdownEsm, { options: ["a", "b", "c"], value: "a" })()
```

Initial state: `{ options: ["a", "b", "c"], value: "a" }`.

### HTML — output/display (ipywidgets: `HTML`, `Label`, `Output`)

`examples/widgets/html.js` — renders `value` as HTML; the host pushes updates, the
widget never writes back.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
htmlEsm = `
export default {
  render({ model, el }) {
    const div = document.createElement("div");
    const paint = () => { div.innerHTML = model.get("value") ?? ""; };
    paint();
    model.on("change:value", paint);
    el.appendChild(div);
    return () => div.remove();
  },
};
`
```

```{ojs}
html = demo(htmlEsm, { value: "<b>hi</b>" })()
```

Initial state: `{ value: "<b>hi</b>" }`. This is kernel→view-only: nothing on screen
writes back to the host, so there is nothing to click — the interesting part is that a
real backend can push `set_state({"value": "<i>bye</i>"})` at any time and this box
updates without a re-render.

## Real ipywidgets controls (no reimplementation required)

Everything above is a *build-your-own* widget: cositos' own minimal ESM, wired through
`AnyModel`. There's a second path, for when you don't want to build a widget at all — you
want the **real** `@jupyter-widgets/controls` frontend (`IntSlider`, `Dropdown`, `VBox`,
…), unmodified, with **no `ipywidgets` (or `anywidget`) Python package installed**.

`cositos.contrib.controls` builds the state for these real widgets directly — it's a
small JSON catalog (their own `_model_name`/`_model_module`/`_view_name`/`_view_module`
identity, pinned at `@jupyter-widgets/controls`/`base` version `2.0.0`) plus a thin
Python builder. This needs **zero changes to cositos' core**: `Widget.send_state` already
sends whatever identity the caller's state carries, so a real widget's own identity
reaches the wire exactly like an anywidget-authored one would.

The slider and dropdown below are rendered by the real `@jupyter-widgets/html-manager`
(loaded from the CDN, same as [static export](tutorials/static-export.qmd)) — not by the
LocalChannel/OJS demo harness the six widgets above use, because these are real
ipywidgets controls models, not cositos anywidget models:

```{python}
# | output: asis
from IPython.display import HTML

from cositos.contrib.controls import dropdown, int_slider, vbox
from cositos.embed import embed_snippet
from cositos.serialize import dump_document

entries = vbox(
    children=[
        int_slider(value=42, min=0, max=100),
        dropdown(options=["a", "b", "c"], value="b"),
    ]
)
doc = dump_document(entries)
root_id = entries[0][0]
HTML(embed_snippet(doc, views=[root_id]))
```

Both controls are interactive right here on the page (drag the slider, open the
dropdown) — that interactivity is the real `IntSliderView`/`DropdownView` frontend code
running, the exact same JS a live JupyterLab loads for `ipywidgets.IntSlider()`.

```python
from cositos.contrib.controls import dropdown, int_slider, vbox
from cositos.serialize import dump_document

entries = vbox(
    children=[
        int_slider(value=42, min=0, max=100),
        dropdown(options=["a", "b", "c"], value="b"),
    ]
)
doc = dump_document(entries)  # a normal cositos Document -- save/restore/embed all apply
```

`int_slider`/`dropdown`/`vbox`/`hbox` each return a `list[ModelEntry]`: the widget's own
entry first, then a freshly id'd entry per companion model it needs (`LayoutModel`,
`SliderStyleModel`, `DescriptionStyleModel`) — every call mints fresh companion ids, so
building the same widget twice never collides on `model_id`. Pass the result straight to
`dump_document`, exactly like any other cositos widget tree.

On a live kernel, the same `entries[0]` state dict opens through the ordinary
`Widget`/`CommTransport` pair (Python) — nothing about `Widget.open()` changes; only the
state you hand it does.

## Next steps

- Full per-language launch walkthrough (all five backends, current live/blocked status):
  [`tutorials/quickstart.qmd`](tutorials/quickstart.qmd).
- Author a brand-new widget (not one of the six above):
  [`tutorials/authoring-widgets.qmd`](tutorials/authoring-widgets.qmd).
- Why these six cover the whole ipywidgets surface: [`widgets.md`](widgets.md).
- The full build-download-restore walkthrough for a dashboard built from the real controls catalog above: [`tutorials/dashboard.qmd`](tutorials/dashboard.qmd).
